content:
10 min read
Just now

--

Most “AI for sales” demos are a chatbot bolted onto a CRM. You ask it a question, it does a lookup, you get an answer. That’s fine. It’s also not the interesting work, and it’s not where the value is.
The interesting work is the recurring report nobody enjoys producing. Take RevOps. Every Monday a small operations team hand-assembles a pipeline briefing for each regional sales leader: open deals, where they stand against quota, what slipped, what’s gone quiet. It’s high-value, it’s grunt work, and because a few people build it by hand, the leaders don’t see it until Tuesday.
That’s the thing to automate. Not because it’s flashy, but because it’s a defined process that runs the same way every week and currently eats a specialist’s morning. The win isn’t “a bot you can ask things.” The win is the briefing shows up in thirty seconds, on demand, and the ops team gets their Monday back.
I’ve built this shape of system more than once now, and the same design decisions come up every time. This is me writing them down so I stop re-deriving them.
A process, not a question
Here’s the line I draw. It’s an agent when the work is a process with steps, state, decisions, and tools, not a single turn. For the briefing the process is explicit and ordered: scope the data to whoever is asking, run the exception logic against that scoped data, then assemble a fixed-format output. A second workflow handles the deep dive (“tell me about this specific deal”), with its own access check.
Two choices made this hold up.
The steps are written as explicit ordered logic, not vibes. Tell a model “give a pipeline briefing” and it free-associates a decent-looking answer. Give it a numbered process with a trigger, inputs, conditional branches, and a required output format, and it executes a process. The difference shows up the moment something is ambiguous. A process has defined behavior. A vibe has a coin flip.
And it’s portable on purpose. I built and tested this as a system prompt plus inlined knowledge, with no dependency on any one vendor’s workflow builder or function-calling format. Visual builders are great right up until you have to move the thing, and then a flow you exported from one platform runs on exactly that platform and nowhere else. A process defined in the prompt runs anywhere there’s a model and a code tool. That portability is also what let me test it across model tiers, which turned out to matter more than I expected.
Where the LLM stops
This is the part people get wrong, so it’s the part I’ll spend the most words on.
An LLM is not a calculator. The moment you let it act like one, it will quietly cost you a deal.
The briefing has exactly one number the agent has to compute itself: the open-pipeline total. Everything else, the published quota rollups, coverage, weighted pipeline, already exists as an official figure that leadership quotes. So the agent reports those. It does not recompute them. But the open-pipeline total has no published source, so the agent has to produce it.
Early on I let the model sum the rows. It looked fine. Then on a weaker model it silently dropped a row, two deals shared an owner and it merged them by eye, and it reported one fewer deal than existed with a total off by a few hundred thousand. The ugly part: its own internal sanity check passed, because the subtotals it added were self-consistent. It was confidently, checkably wrong.
Sit with why a cross-check doesn’t save you here. “The total must equal the sum of the subtotals” catches a total that disagrees with its parts. It cannot catch a subtotal that’s wrong because a row never entered the calculation in the first place. You can’t validate your way out of a missing input.
The fix is not a smarter model. The fix is to stop letting the model do the arithmetic at all. The aggregate gets computed in the platform’s code tool: filter the full table in code, sum in code, return the count, the total, the by-stage breakdown, and the list of record IDs that went into the sum so a human can see nothing was dropped. The model’s only job is to decide what to compute and to call the tool. The number is now deterministic and model-independent, because the model isn’t the one adding.
Stated generally:

• Published rollups get reported, never recomputed. The system of record is the source of truth, and the agent is not a second opinion on numbers leadership already quotes.

• Anything without a published figure gets computed in code, with an explicit row-by-row fallback where no tool exists, flagged as agent-computed.

• The LLM decides what to check. It never decides what the number is.
This leans on one assumption: a standard agent platform with basic code execution. That’s a cheap, defensible floor. Compare it to the alternative, “we need a frontier model so the mental math is usually right,” which isn’t a fix at all. It’s a bet, and you’re making it again every time traffic gets routed to whatever model is cheap that day.
It generalizes well past RevOps. Any agent that touches money, counts, dates, or eligibility has the same fault line. Find the figures with an authoritative source and report them. Find the ones without and compute them in code. Never let the language model be the ledger.
The rules that beat helpfulness
A grounded agent sitting in front of an exec needs hard rules that override its instinct to be useful. The ones that recur on every build like this:
Scope before any data touch. A regional leader sees their region. A manager sees their own reports. Anyone the system can’t place sees nothing and gets routed to a human. This is the first step of every workflow, not a filter slapped on at the end. Cross-boundary requests get declined by rule, with the broad-scope phrasings (“show me the other region,” “everything company-wide”) named explicitly so the model doesn’t talk itself into an exception.
Refusals framed as policy, not absence. Sensitive fields like compensation, margin, and discount get refused as policy, not as “I couldn’t find that.” The distinction is deliberate. An “I don’t have it” refusal evaporates the day someone adds the field to the dataset. A policy refusal holds regardless of what’s in the data, so you pre-write refusals even for fields that aren’t present yet but that users will predictably ask for.
No editorializing about people. The agent reports data. It does not rate, rank, or compare individuals, and it never speculates about why someone’s numbers look the way they do. “How is this rep doing versus the team?” gets declined. That’s not squeamishness, it’s scope. The moment an agent starts judging people from a data snapshot, it’s making claims it can’t ground and that someone will, correctly, be furious about.
Say what you don’t know, out loud. The agent can report that a deal moved and when. It cannot explain why, because it doesn’t have the conversation the rep had with the customer. So it says so, on the call, instead of inventing a plausible reason. There’s a dedicated step in the deep-dive workflow for exactly that boundary.
Escalation that preserves context verbatim. When the agent hands off, it carries the exact identifiers copied verbatim from the source row, plus the requester’s exact question. A handoff that mangles a record ID or swaps in the wrong account name is worse than no handoff. This was a real failure I caught in testing: a model paraphrased an identifier on the way out. The fix was an explicit “copy these fields verbatim” instruction, and it held.
Flag the bad data. Don’t fix it.
Real operational data is dirty, and a grounded agent that doesn’t notice will repeat the dirt in its own confident voice. So the stance is simple: surface problems as stated assumptions and flags, never as silent corrections.
The things that turn up: records that contradict themselves, a stage that implies recent activity sitting next to a blank activity date, a snapshot whose label disagrees with the dates inside it, rollups that don’t reconcile with the rows beneath them. The agent flags each one with the evidence and a “route this to the data team,” and keeps going. It does not guess the missing value, and it does not quietly patch a number to make things tie out.
This is a trust thing. An agent that silently fixes data is an agent you can’t trust to tell you when something’s broken. One that flags is one a data owner can actually rely on.
There’s a bigger pattern hiding in here, and it’s the most reusable piece of the whole build. Every grounded-agent project starts the same way. Someone hands you a table and says “build an assistant on this.” Before anyone writes a prompt, a human has to answer three questions about that table by hand. What does each column mean, and which are safe to answer from? Which are sensitive and need scoping or refusal? And what’s wrong in the data that would make the agent confidently lie? That analysis is the actual skill. Under a deadline it’s the first thing that gets skipped.
It’s also automatable, but not with a script. A script validates known expectations: column X is non-null, matches this pattern. It can’t read a never-before-seen schema and infer what a column means, or judge whether a field called “score” is a harmless rating or a sensitive internal metric, or spot an integrity problem that violates no pre-written rule because nobody’s written the rule yet. That kind of semantic reasoning over unfamiliar structure, under business context, is exactly what a model is good at and a script can’t touch. So you build a step that ingests a raw table and emits a reviewable grounding-and-guardrail spec: per-column meaning and answerability, a sensitivity map with concrete refusal rules, integrity flags with severity and evidence. It drafts. A human signs off. Then you build the agent. It never auto-publishes, because a wrong sensitivity call is a data leak.
That turns the slow manual front end of every grounded-agent project into one reviewable pass. It’s the biggest win I took from this.
How you actually know it works
“It seemed to work when I tried it” is not evidence. The agent carries a test record, and the tests are the part I’d defend hardest.
An adversarial probe per guardrail. Every rule gets a probe designed to break it: a cross-boundary data request, a request for a forbidden field, a “rank these people” ask, a “why did this happen” that should trigger a routed handoff instead of a fabricated answer. A guardrail you haven’t tried to break is a guardrail you’re hoping works.
Over-refusal calibration in the other direction. An agent that refuses everything is also broken, just quietly, by being useless. So the suite includes legitimate questions the agent must answer, like “what does this pipeline stage mean?” You’re calibrating a boundary, and a boundary has two sides.
And the one that earned its keep: run it on a weak model on purpose. In a real deployment you don’t always control the model behind the platform. So I ran the agent on a non-frontier tier, not just a strong one. That’s what surfaced the arithmetic failure. The guardrails held everywhere from the first pass, because rule-following is something models do reliably. The one number the model computed itself broke on the weaker tier. That’s an enormously useful signal. It told me precisely which part of the system was leaning on model capability instead of design, and that part is exactly the part I moved into code. Test only on the best model and you ship a system whose correctness depends on which model happens to be serving traffic that day.
The shape of the lesson: rule-following holds across tiers, so guardrails tend to travel well. Computation doesn’t, so every figure needs an authoritative source or a code path. Design your evals to find which is which.
What changes in production, and what doesn’t
A prototype self-states identity (“I’m the East regional lead”) because there’s no auth layer to lean on. That’s the one piece that has to harden befo
